coredata.dc:description: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are considered as potential photocatalyst for H2O2 production. However, stable zirconium-based porphyrin MOFs have not been tried to produce H2O2 due to their fast charge recombination and poor oxygen adsorption. Herein, an efficient binary hybrid photocatalyst was synthesized by in situ encapsulating boron-doped carbon dots (CDs) into PCN-222 via forming B-O-Zr bonds. High H2O2 photogeneration rate of 13.4 mmol g−1 h−1 under Vis-NIR light is achieved from CDs 11D@PCN-222 in seawater, and the photocatalytic performance is 222 and 330 times that of PCN-222 in water and seawater. Theoretical calculations prove that the adjacent-adsorption sites of hydrogen protons and oxygen in photocatalysts are conducive to the accumulation of superoxide radicals and further highly selective proton coupling, and the electron-withdrawing hydroxyl group (B-OH) forces the boron active center to have a better desorption of intermediates (OOH*). The catalytic activity of photocatalyst still maintained 93.8 % after ten cycles.

head.ce:title: Boron-doped carbon dots modified zirconium porphyrin MOFs enable efficient and highly selective photosynthesis of hydrogen peroxide in water and seawater

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Chemistry, Northeast Normal University, Changchun 130024, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Chemistry, Northeast Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Chemistry, Northeast Normal University, Changchun 130024, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are considered as potential photocatalyst for H2O2 production. However, stable zirconium-based porphyrin MOFs have not been tried to produce H2O2 due to their fast charge recombination and poor oxygen adsorption. Herein, an efficient binary hybrid photocatalyst was synthesized by in situ encapsulating boron-doped carbon dots (CDs) into PCN-222 via forming B-O-Zr bonds. High H2O2 photogeneration rate of 13.4 mmol g−1 h−1 under Vis-NIR light is achieved from CDs 11D@PCN-222 in seawater, and the photocatalytic performance is 222 and 330 times that of PCN-222 in water and seawater. Theoretical calculations prove that the adjacent-adsorption sites of hydrogen protons and oxygen in photocatalysts are conducive to the accumulation of superoxide radicals and further highly selective proton coupling, and the electron-withdrawing hydroxyl group (B-OH) forces the boron active center to have a better desorption of intermediates (OOH*). The catalytic activity of photocatalyst still maintained 93.8 % after ten cycles.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: In situ encapsulation of boron-doped CDs with PCN-222 via B-O-Zr bonds achieves a stable photocatalyst CDs11D@PCN-222 with better Vis-NIR light absorption for efficient H2O2 photosynthesis. The strong adsorption of O2 and H+ on the adjacent active sites and convenient proton coupling path endows the photocatalyst with considerable H2O2 yield in water and seawater.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, photocatalytic hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) production has become a new research direction [1]. Compared with the traditional process, photocatalysis is an effective means to produce H2O2 due to its advantages of less energy consumption, non-toxic by-products and simple operation [2,3]. Since 2010, a variety molecule-based and semiconductor-based photocatalysts have emerged for the generation of H2O2 [4], such as ZnCdS [5], cobalt chlorin (CoII (Ch)) [6], C3N4 [7,8], TiO2 [9], rGO [10], BiVO4 [11], metal nanoparticles [12,13], etc. In order to improve catalytic activity, these components are often hybridized with other semiconductors [14]. However, the stability, oxygen adsorption capacity and catalytic performance of the reported photocatalysts still need to be upgraded.

The metal organic frameworks (MOFs), characterized by their large specific surface area, certain pore channels, diverse structures, more active sites and structure adjustability, have been extensively investigated in the field of photocatalysis [15]. Yamashita group pioneered the report on the MOFs-based catalysts NiO/MIL-125-NH2 for H2O2 photogeneration [16]. However, MIL-125-NH2 cannot be recycled due to its poor water stability. Subsequently, a series of MOF-based photocatalysts were developed by hydrophobic engineering of MOFs or doping appropriate metal ions into oxo clusters of MOFs [17–20]. In addition, the photocatalysts are also constructed using diverse strategies including functionalizing organic linkers, building heterojunctions, linker defect engineering in the MOFs, etc [21–29]. Photocatalytic generation of H2O2 typically involves two mechanisms: oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and water oxidation reaction (WOR) [30]. The 4e- ORR pathway and 2e- ORR are proposed based on the electron transfer numbers and reaction intermediates involved in H2O2 generation. Whatever the reaction routes, it is crucial to improve the stability, adsorption of oxygen, and light absorption of the photocatalysts [31]. Unfortunately, the oxygen adsorption capacity of all MOF based catalysts is very limited [32]. In 2021, Liu group synthesized boron-containing UiO-66 by replacing partial p-phthalic acid ligands with 4-carboxyphenylboronic acid [33]. High-selective proton coupling dual-electron transfer and excellent oxygen adsorption, efficient electron transmission and fast H2O2 generating are realized in this report. Nevertheless, the limited light absorption range of MOFs selected in the research of photocatalytic generation of H2O2 is not conducive to the full conversion and utilization of light energy [34,35].

Zirconium porphyrin MOFs has excellent optical properties and strong stability [36]. Among them, PCN-222 with large pore structure (3.7 nm) is resistant to strong acid and water, and has become a potential material for research in the field of photocatalysis [37–40]. However, because of the limited lifetime and rapid recombination of photogenerated carriers, the PCN-222 is often hybridized with the guest materials to ameliorate the transmission efficiency of photogenerated electrons and holes [41,42]. The excellent properties of the zirconium porphyrin MOFs are expected to become the most potential choice for photocatalytic synthesis of H2O2. However, the results based on the current research of our group indicate that the photocatalytic performance of PCN-222 to generate H2O2 is inefficient (27.47 μmol g−1 h−1). And another key constraint on photoproduction of H2O2 using PCN-222 as photocatalyst are its limited adsorption capacity of oxygen and the low utilization of light energy [43]. Therefore, encapsulating suitable guests into PCN-222 to achieve binary hybrid synergy through confinement is an effective means to solve the above problems [44,45].

Carbon dots (CDs), as potential carbon-based guest materials, have excellent photoluminescence, biocompatibility, electron transfer ability, and are easy to synthesize due to their wide range of carbon sources [46]. The utilization of CDs in photocatalytic H2O2 production has been reported due to their ability to stabilize and store photogenerated electrons, facilitate efficient charge separation, and enhance the catalyst's photocatalytic activity and stability [47,48]. The heteroatom doping in CDs can effectively regulate the local chemical characteristics of graphene structure and its band gaps, leading to the changes in electrical and optical properties [49–51]. Boron is the only non-metallic element with less than four electrons in the outermost layer, and the empty P orbit makes the boron highly electronegative. Moreover, inspired by the report of the Li group [33], the boron modification of photocatalyst can greatly improve the properties of oxygen and proton adsorption, and further increase the yield of hydrogen peroxide. However, CDs are difficult to recycle for photocatalysis and have limited photocatalytic ability, so CDs are often hybridized with other components to improve the efficiency and selectivity of H2O2 generation [52–54]. Therefore, it is expected that PCN-222 can be modified by selecting suitable CDs with special functional groups and excellent optical properties, thereby boosting the photocatalytic performance of PCN-222 as a photocatalyst for H2O2 generation.

Herein, a binary photocatalyst with wide light absorption, high oxygen adsorption capability, fast carrier transport efficiency and efficient photocatalytic production of H2O2 was constructed by in situ encapsulating boron-doped carbon dots in PCN-222 channels. Compared with pure PCN-222, the hybrid catalyst has better adsorption capability for oxygen and hydrogen protons. The different carbon dots doped with boron exerts a significant influence on the performance of the resulted photocatalysts. The H2O2 evolution rates of CDs 11D@PCN-222 in water and seawater are 222 and 330 times higher than those of the pristine PCN-222, respectively. The H2O2 production is further enhanced in the presence of the proton donor, and this is unprecedented in the catalysts reported so far. The density functional theory (DFT) calculation verified the rationality of the design of binary photocatalyst. The present study offers a novel approach for the construction of efficient photocatalysts for photosynthesis of H2O2 by fully utilizing solar energy.

ZrCl4 (0.05 g) and different masses of CDs 11 (0.2 g, 0.3 g, 0.4 g, 0.5 g, and 0.6 g) were added in a 50 mL three-necked flask containing 10 mL DMF, stirred for 10 min, and then 0.05 g TCPP, 0.5 mL H2O and 1.2 mL acetic acid were added. The homogeneous solution was heated in a 120 °C oil bath for 15 min, cooled to ambient temperature afterwards. The products were centrifuged and then washed with water/ethanol and dried. The catalysts were named CDs 11 A@PCN-222, CDs 11B@PCN-2222, CDs 11 C@PCN-222, CDs 11D@PCN-222 and CDs 11E@PCN-222, respectively. And the CDs 1@PCN-222, CDs 2@PCN-222, CDs 3@PCN-222, CDs 4@PCN-222, CDs 5@PCN-222, CDs 6@PCN-222, CDs 7@PCN-222, CDs 8@PCN-222, CDs 9@PCN-222, CDs 10 @PCN-222 and CDs 12@PCN-222 were also synthesized in the same way.

15 mg of as-synthesized photocatalyst was dispersed into a beaker containing 50 mL deionized water or a proton donor aqueous solution (10 %). The suspension solution was stirred for 30 min in the dark with continually O2 bubbling to reach the absorption-desorption equilibrium. A 300 W xenon lamp (PLS-SXE300/300UV) with a 420 nm cut-off filter was used as the light source. The optical density is 50 mW cm−2 measured by the optical power meter (CEAULIOHT, CEL-FZ-A). And continuous magnetic stirring and cooling water (15 °C) were applied during the experiment. During illumination, 2 mL solution was sampled every 20 min and centrifuged to remove the photocatalyst.

The amount of H2O2 was analyzed by iodimetry. Typically, 1 mL of 0.1 mol L−1 potassium phthalate monobasic (C8H5KO4) and 1 mL of 0.4 mol L−1 potassium iodide (KI) aqueous solutions were added to the obtained solution, which stand still for 30 min. The amount of H2O2 was determined by measuring the absorption intensity at 350 nm using UV–vis spectra.

The twelve CDs were synthesized from different carbon sources according to previous reports (Table S1) [47–50]. The CDs 6–9 were not doped with boron atoms, and the other CDs were synthesized from different carbon sources and boron sources. The light absorption properties of twelve CDs were measured by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (Fig. S1). The absorption edges of these CDs are almost in the visible light region, whereas that of CDs 3, CDs 5, CDs 6, CDs 8, and CDs 10–12 can extend up to NIR light region, which is in line with the previous reports [51,55–62]. In the synthesis process of CDs, the carbonization temperature will change the absorption capacity of CDs [63,64]. The photocatalyst with better light absorption usually promote a significant improvement in catalytic performance. The CDs with excellent optical properties as guest are crucial to the carrier as host. In addition, the bonds and functional groups that facilitate electron transport in the CDs structure will also give the CDs great potential in photocatalysis [65]. Furthermore, the modification ability of PCN-222 by different CDs was evaluated.

The binary hybrid catalysts were synthesized by loading CDs into the pores of PCN-222 by in situ encapsulation method ( Scheme 1). Compared with the PCN-222, the modification of PCN-222 by twelve CDs is very successful, and the photocatalytic performance of hydrogen peroxide production is greatly improved (Fig. S2). This is due to the CDs act as electron acceptor to inhibit the recombination of photogenerated charges and holes of PCN-222, and the abundant functional groups on the CDs surface promote the dispersion of the photocatalyst in water [66], proving that our synthetic strategy is general and reasonable. Among the numerous catalyst-wrapped CDs, CDs 6–9 are boron-free. Interestingly, the photocatalytic performance of these several CDs-modified catalysts is inefficient. We preliminarily deduced that the electron-deficient character of boron atom has a positive effect on the activity of the photocatalyst. This is derived from the electron-deficient nature of boron atom, which is conducive to the adsorption of oxygen and protons [33]. However, the modification of PCN-222 with boron doping CDs 1, 2 and 4 is not very significant owing to their weaker light absorption in visible-light region. Therefore, the doping of boron atoms is not the decisive factor to improve the catalyst performance, and the optical absorption of the guest is also important. Moreover, CDs 3 and CDs 5 are both boron-doped and good light-absorbing guests, but they are not the best choices for PCN-222. On the contrary, CDs 10–12 synthesized with phloroglucinol and boric acid were encapsulated in the pores of MOF to obtain better catalytic results. Therefore, the CDs synthesized from carbon source with aromatic ring structure is more favorable to the electron transport between host and guest in the photocatalyst [67]. In the synthesis process of CDs 10–12, the molar ratio of phloroglucinol to boric acid is 4:1, 1:1 and 1:4, respectively. It has been reported that the fluorescence of CDs synthesized with phloroglucinol and boric acid is highly dependent on the concentration of doped B. As the B doping content is low, these luminescent centers are dispersed and diluted [51]. But, as the concentration of B is too high, the concentration quenching effect will take place, causing the entire fluorescence to decay. So, we have screened out the best guest which is CDs 11. Therefore, the excellent guest property of CDs 11 requires both phenylboronic acid and phloroglucinol, which are indispensable. And the synergistic effect between the different optical and physical properties of CDs and MOF determines the H2O2 production directly. Significantly, the photocatalyst formed by combining CDs 11 and PCN-222 has the excellent photocatalytic effect, and the H2O2 generation rate is 2206.80 μmol g−1 h−1 within 80 min in water, which is 80 times higher than that of both PCN-222 and CDs 11. Besides, the CDs 11@PCN-222 cannot be excited to generate electrons in the dark, so no hydrogen peroxide was detected, which also proves that hydrogen peroxide is generated through a photocatalytic process in this work (Fig. S2a). Therefore, the subsequent experiments will focus on CDs 11@PCN-222 as the main research target.

The scanning electron microscopy morphology (SEM) image of the binary hybrid CDs 11@PCN-222 is almost the same as PCN-222, and the size of the nanoparticles is about 225 nm ( Fig. 1a-b). The diameter of CDs 11 is about 3.1 nm (Fig. S3a), and its lattice spacing is 0.23 nm, which is consistent with the reported result (Fig. 1c) [51], and the size of CDs 11 matches with the channel size (3.7 nm) of PCN-222. The CDs 11 encapsulated in CDs 11@PCN-222 is no different from pure CDs 11 (Fig. 1d), indicating that CDs 11 was successfully encapsulated in the MOF channels (Fig. S4). And the elemental mapping result shows the CDs 11 is evenly distributed in the MOF channels (Fig. S5). The catalyst surface chemical element composition was measured by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) ( Fig. 2). The atomic percentage of CDs 11 is 3.81 % for B, 77.84 % for C and 18.35 % for O. Further, the CDs 11D@PCN-222 catalyst contains 0.67 % B, 81.58 % C, 2.27 % N, 13.96 % O and 1.52 % Zr, and the results are close to the energy dispersion X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) characterization (Table S2), which proves that the CDs 11 are successfully combined with PCN-222. In the XPS spectra of B 1 s (Fig. 2b), the peaks at 193.45 eV, 192.66 eV and 191.00 eV are attributed to B-O, B-CO2 and B-C bonds in CDs 11, respectively [68,69]. The B-C and B-CO2 can enhance redox ability of photocatalysts [70]. Compared with CDs 11, the binding energy of B 1 s in CDs 11D@PCN-222 is shifted to lower position. In O 1 s spectra of PCN-222 (Fig. 2c), the O-CO, C-O, CO separated peaks are located at 533.66 eV, 532.15 eV and 531.12 eV, respectively. However, compared with CDs 11, the binding energy of O 1 s in CDs 11@PCN-222 is shifted to higher position, and the new peak in O 1 s of CDs 11D@PCN-222 belongs to B-O-Zr lattice oxygen [33], which proves the successful synthesis of a binary hybrid photocatalyst of CDs 11 and PCN-222. It is inferred that the B-OH in CDs 11 are coordinated with zirconium clusters at synthetic temperature, which is conducive to the photogenic electron transport between CDs 11 and PCN-222. Besides, the Zr 3d3/2 and Zr 3d5/2 peaks of CDs 11D@PCN-222 at 185.20 eV and 182.87 eV are shifted to higher position compared to PCN-222 (Fig. 2d). In conclusion, it is speculated that there are fewer electrons on PCN-222 and more electrons are transferred to the surface of CDs 11 in the hybrid photocatalyst CDs11D@PCN-222 [71]. The bridge of electron transfer is the B-O-Zr bond formed by two single component materials. Many catalysts have been reported where CDs are in situ encased in pores of MOF, but the electron transfer via covalent bonding is often efficient compared to interfacial electron transfer [72,73]. Therefore, the photocatalyst we designed and synthesized is reasonable and worthy of expectation.

The fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy always provides information about what chemical bonds or functional groups are present in material. From FTIR analysis of CDs 11, the absorption band at 3235 cm−1 is corresponding to O-H stretching vibration ( Fig. 3a). The B-O stretching vibration is observed at 1339 cm−1, and the peak at 1189 cm−1 is attributed to an asymmetrically stretched oxygen atom connecting the trigonal boron atoms [51]. Moreover, the bands at 1699, 1591 and 1386 cm−1 are owing to the CO and CC bond stretching vibrations in CDs 11, PCN-222 and CDs 11@PCN-222. Besides, a new band at 939 cm−1 is originated from B-C bond in CDs 11@PCN-222, owing to the doping of CDs [68]. Additionally, the 10 cm−1 shift of Zr-O peak due to the formation of B-O-Zr bond is effective evidence for binding of PCN-222 and CDs 11 [74]. The above results indicate that there are abundant functional groups on the boron-doped CDs 11 surface, including BCO2 bond especially, and the CDs 11 is effectively combined with PCN-222 by B-O-Zr bond to form a stable binary hybrid catalyst.

The crystal structures of the photocatalysts were analyzed by powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) patterns (Fig. 3b). A broad peak is observed near 2θ=24°, corresponding to the typical (002) plane of graphite structure in CDs 11 [51]. Some sharp peaks belong to the PCN-222, and a diffraction peak originated from the CDs also exists in CDs 11@PCN-222. In general, the crystal morphology of PCN-222 did not change, proving that the MOF was not destroyed in the loading process with the CDs 11. Multi-component construction of photocatalyst usually improves light absorption property and obtains a narrow band gap energy. UV–vis-NIR diffuse reflectance spectra show that the hybridization of CDs 11 and PCN-222 greatly broadens the light absorption region of the photocatalyst up to 1400 nm. And the absorption onsets for CDs 11 and PCN-222 are calculated to be 892 nm (1.39 eV) and 708.6 nm (1.75 eV) respectively (Fig. 3c). This also explains that CDs 11 with narrow band gap can effectively regulate the optical properties of MOFs. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area of PCN-222 and CDs 11@PCN-222 are calculated from the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms to be 2221 m2 g−1 and 186 m2 g−1, respectively (Fig. 3d) [75]. The significant decrease of nitrogen adsorption capacity shows that CDs occupied the pores of MOFs, which once again indicates the successful preparation of the CDs 11@PCN-222. The preserved pores ensure the diffusion of O2, reactive species, and product in the photocatalyst channels.

In order to explore the charge transfer path, the fluorescence quenching behavior of the photocatalyst was observed by steady-state photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy ( Fig. 4a). The PL intensity of CDs 11D@PCN-222 is lower than that of PCN-222, indicating that the combination of CDs 11 and PCN-222 caused charge transfer [76,77]. Moreover, the PL emission center at about 620 nm for the CDs 11 is independent on the excitation wavelength (Fig. 4b). Furthermore, the average lifetimes (τave) of PCN-222 and CDs 11D@PCN-222 were determined from the time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) decay spectra to be 1.67 ns and 4.84 ns with emission at 650 nm, and 1.65 ns and 3.18 ns with emission at 725 nm, respectively (Fig. 4c-d). The τave of CDs 11 was calculated as 2.71 ns (Fig.S3b). Compared with CDs 11 and PCN-222, the increased τave of CDs 11D@PCN-222 is attribured to the extension of long decay component (τ2) (Table S3), proving that the formation of B-O-Zr bond contributes to prolong the photogenerated charge lifetime, which is beneficial to the improvement of photocarrier transport efficiency and photocatalytic performance [78,79]. Comprehensive analysis of XPS spectra and PL spectra show that the transfer of photogenerated electrons is from PCN-222 to CDs 11, which is because the electron-deficient B-doped CDs as good electron acceptor can further promote the efficiency of electron transfer. The strength of the photocurrent curve is positively correlated with the separation of charges and holes generated by the photoexcitation of the photocatalyst. The photocurrent signal of the CDs 11D@PCN-222 is significantly higher than that of CDs 11 and PCN-222, so the binary hybrid has better photogenerated carrier transfer efficiency (Fig. 4c). And the EIS Nyquist plots (Fig. 4d) show that CDs 11D@PCN-222 has the smallest semicircle radius, proving enhanced charge transfer ability.

A series of samples of CDs 11x@PCN-222 (x=A, B, C, D, E) were synthesized by adding different masses of CDs 11 (0.2 g, 0.3 g, 0.4 g, 0.5 g, and 0.6 g) into the reaction systems. The photocatalytic control experiment of H2O2 generation was carried out in water to verify whether the CDs 11@PCN-222 has efficient photocatalytic activity. The H2O2 generation efficiencies of PCN-222, CDs 11 A@PCN-222 CDs, 11B@PCN-222, CDs 11 C@PCN-222, CDs 11D@PCN-222, CDs 11E@PCN-222 under Vis-NIR (420–1100 nm) light irradiation are 27.47 μmol g−1 h−1, 1090.67 μmol g−1 h−1, 2206.80 μmol g−1 h−1, 4241.49 μmol g−1 h−1, 6107.75 μmol g−1 h−1, 5037.32 μmol g−1 h−1 within 80 min, respectively ( Fig. 5a). The photocatalytic performance of CDs 11D@PCN-222 is 222 times that of PCN-222. The highly efficient catalytic efficiency of the CDs 11D@PCN-222 is not only higher than most of the photocatalysts that have been reported to produce H2O2 (Table S4), but also further illustrates that the CDs 11 and PCN-222 have an excellent synergistic effect. Therefore, the screened CDs 11D@PCN-222 was used as photocatalyst in subsequent experiments.

In oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), the oxidation potential of many catalysts is insufficient to oxidize water, so it usually is necessary to add hole (h+) scavengers to promote the formation of products in photocatalytic reactions. The several common hole sacrificial agents, such as isopropanol (IPA), ethanol (EtOH), benzyl alcohol (BnOH), tert-butanol (TBA) and methyl alcohol (MeOH) were added to explore their influence on the H2O2 production (Fig. 5b). The H2O2 generation rates in the presence of these sacrificial agents are 8331.08 μmol g−1 h−1, 8971.70 μmol g−1 h−1, 17094.22 μmol g−1 h−1, 6216.25 μmol g−1 h−1, and 6431.15 μmol g−1 h−1, respectively. According to the analysis, the reactive species in the photocatalytic mechanism herein do not contain h+ and hydroxyl radical (•OH). Moreover, in the absence of photocatalyst, the hydrogen peroxide generated by high false-positive yields of peroxides from benzyl alcohol is negligible. Interestingly, compared with other hole sacrificial agents, BnOH has a better promoting effect on the catalytic performance of CDs 11D@PCN-222, and its catalytic yield is 2.80 times that of the pure water without sacrificial agent. BnOH is immiscible with water, and the oxidation reaction of benzyl alcohol by holes generates benzaldehyde, thereby rapidly consuming useless h+ to promote the formation of H2O2 [17,18]. It is a common method in chemical reactions to remove the product, and the separation of BnOH and benzaldehyde with water promotes the acquisition of H2O2. And BnOH and benzaldehyde are easily separated from water, avoiding the contamination of the aqueous phase by the electron/proton donor. This proves that the CDs 11D@PCN-222 designed in this work has efficient photocatalytic performance, providing ideas for the production process of H2O2.

Superoxide radicals (•O2 -) exist more stably under low-pH aqueous phase, thereby further promoting the generation of H2O2 [17]. However, the framework of most MOFs materials will be destroyed in the acidic system, and they cannot maintain a complete chemical structure. Zirconium-based porphyrin MOFs have good stability in acidic, weak alkaline or boiling water solution, which makes it widely applicable to various research fields [80]. Therefore, the photoproduction of H2O2 catalyzed by CDs 11D@PCN-222 was monitored in different pH aqueous phase (Fig. 5c). As the pH values are 9, 7, 5, 2 and 1, the rate of H2O2 produced increases from 3644.24 μmol g−1 h−1 to 14431.11 μmol g−1 h−1 with decrease in pH values. Therefore, the acidic solution is rich in hydrogen protons and can stabilize the superoxide radicals. The synergy between PCN-222 and CDs 11 is extremely impressive, including efficient photogenerated carrier transport and excellent optical properties. It also shows that PCN-222, a potential carrier, has good stability even in strong acidic solution, and SEM and XRD characterization also confirmed this fact (Fig. S6).

The concentration of catalyst in the photocatalytic process directly determines the maximum accumulation of the H2O2. By optimizing the concentration of CDs 11D@PCN-222 photocatalyst, the H2O2 yield of 40 mg catalyst can be further increased by 2.53 times to 6176.41 μM as compared with that of 15 mg catalyst (Fig. 5d). However, when the concentration of CDs 11D@PCN-222 was further increased to 50 mg and 60 mg, the accumulation of H2O2 showed a decrease trend, which was due to the increasement of light scattering caused by the increase of suspension concentration, resulting in the decrease of light penetration into the photocatalyst suspension and the catalyst activity [81]. Therefore, the controlled experiment proves that the dosage of 40 mg CDs 11D@PCN-222 is the best choice.

In the entire photocatalytic system, the oxygen is the main source of reactive species, so the content of dissolved oxygen in water is positively correlated with the production of H2O2. Therefore, the photocatalytic synthesis experiment of H2O2 after bubbling O2, air, N2 with water and catalyst was performed for 30 min in the dark ( Fig. 6a). The H2O2 yields are 6176 μM, 2859 μM and 413 μM, respectively. We speculate that the generation of H2O2 after bubbling N2 is derived from the oxygen in the residual air. Therefore, the formation of H2O2 was not detected with continuous nitrogen flow (Fig. 6b). Accompanied by the continuous introduction of oxygen into the water, the H2O2 obtained is as high as 8462 μM. This clearly shows the importance of oxygen and that the CDs 11D@PCN-222 cannot oxidize water to produce oxygen.

In addition, the active oxygen species trapping experiments show that the superoxide radical is the main active species in the photocatalytic process ( Fig. 7a). The VB of CDs 11D@PCN-222 is insufficient to oxidize water to produce H2O2 (1.76 V vs NHE) and •OH (2.73 V vs NHE), besides, the hydroxyl radicals need to reach a certain concentration to directly generate H2O2. Here, we have ruled out the possibility of 1O2, •OH and h+ participating in H2O2 formation and further demonstrated the indispensability of oxygen. Therefore, the reaction mechanism in this work is selective two-electron reduction of O2. The average number of electrons (n) of the PCN-222 and CDs 11D@PCN-222 for H2O2 generation was measured by the rotating ring-disk electrode (RRDE). The formulas for the ring current and the disk current can be calculated to obtain the n of transferred electrons of PCN-222 and CDs 11D@PCN-222 as 2.35 and 2.08, respectively (Fig. 7c) [33]. In this context, the corresponding H2O2 selectivity are 83 % and 96 %, indicating that CDs 11D@PCN-222 is more efficient for H2O2 evolution (Fig. 7d). However, the selective two-electron reduction is divided into direct two-electron mechanism (Eq. 1) and an indirect two-electron mechanism. The electron spin resonance (ESR) detection of •O2 - is an effective way to distinguish the two mechanisms. The signals of •O2 - were found with 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as the trapping agent after Vis-NIR light irradiation (Fig. 7b). Therefore, the photocatalytic H2O2 production process by CDs 11D@PCN-222 is an indirect two-electron mechanism, that is, the formation of •O2 - by oxygen and photogenerated electrons, and further with H+ and electrons to generate H2O2 (Eqs. 2 and 3).
(1) O2 + 2H+ + 2e- → H2O2 (+0.68V vs. NHE)
(2) O2 + e- → •O2 - (0.33V vs. NHE)
(3)
•O2 - + 2H+ + e- → H2O2 (+1.44V vs. NHE)

Sodium ions (Na+) have been shown to stabilize •O2 - through complexation, thereby inhibiting the occurrence of side reactions between •O2 - and H2O2 [17]. Seawater, which accounts for 97 % of the earth's total water, is rich in salts, among which sodium is the most abundant metal element in seawater, accounting for about 4 % [82]. The production of substances valuable to human from seawater have broad development prospects. In this context, we have employed seawater to investigate the generation of H2O2. The generation of H2O2 photocatalyzed by CDs 11D@PCN-222 in seawater is 14238.8 μM (2.33 times over water) within 80 min, and the reaction trend follows first-order kinetic ( Fig. 8a), confirming the hypothesis above, also further shows that the CDs 11D@PCN-222 is very effective in utilizing seawater for photocatalytic production of H2O2. However, many soluble impurities in seawater affect the H2O2 generation, so we explored the excellent characteristics of Na+ with saturated sodium chloride aqueous solution. Since there is no impurity in the reaction system and the concentration of Na+ is saturated, the product formation rate is 20260.7 μM (3.32 times over water). The key role of Na+ endows the Kf and Kd of hydrogen peroxide in seawater as 198.15 μM min−1 and 0.00281 min−1. In saturated sodium chloride aqueous solution, the Kf is 248.05 μM min−1 and the Kd is almost 0 min−1, indicating that the Na+ in seawater is indeed co-catalysts for the reaction (Fig. 8b). The H2O2 evolution rate over CDs 11D@PCN-222 is 330 times that over PCN-222 in seawater (Fig. S7). In addition, we have also done experiments on the decomposition of H2O2 by the photocatalyst. It can be seen from Fig. 9a that CDs 11D@PCN-222 has a smaller decomposition rate, which further illustrates the applicability of CDs 11D@PCN-222 to this field. From the above results, the premise of ensuring the long-term stability of the photocatalyst is that cheap and easy-to-obtain seawater is bound to become a substitute of water for H2O2 photosynthesis. Fortunately, the CDs 11D@PCN-222 we designed is very stable in seawater and water, and the photocatalytic activity maintained 93.8 % (12543 μmol g−1 h−1) and 96.5 % (5589 μmol g−1 h−1) after ten cycles (Fig. 8c and Fig. S8). In addition, the long-term reaction of CDs 11D@PCN-222 for H2O2 accumulation show that the H2O2 photoproduction is up to 65 mM within 6 h in seawater (Fig. S9). The excellent stability of the CDs 11D@PCN-222 benefits from the protection of the CDs 11 by PCN-222, and the formation of B-O-Zr bond is crucial to the stability of the structure. Furthermore, the H2O2 production yield obtained by the photocatalyst under natural sunlight irradiation is almost the same as that under a xenon lamp irradiation in seawater (Fig. 9b-c). It fully demonstrates that the effective combination of boron-doped CDs with MOFs is reasonable and feasible.

Our designed strategy for the photosynthesis of H2O2 by CDs11@PCN-222 is generally applicable for other Zr-MOFs. To verify this, we selected several zirconium-based MOFs, such as UiO-66, MOF-525, PCN-224 and PCN-223 to encapsulate CDs 11 in situ form CDs 11@MOFs, and explored their photocatalytic performance to generate H2O2 in water/BnOH (Fig. S10). Surprisingly, the hydrogen peroxide yields of the synthesized CDs 11@UiO-66, CDs 11@MOF-525, CDs 11@PCN-224 and CDs 11@PCN-223 were 18.3 (8475 μmol g−1 h−1), 16.1 (15725 μmol g−1 h−1), 16.6 (14547 μmol g−1 h−1), and 16.7 (13997 μmol g−1 h−1) times that of the pure MOFs, respectively. The pores and BET surface areas of different MOFs are different. If the input amounts of CDs 11 and MOFs in the synthesis process is carefully studied, it is expected to further improve the photocatalytic performance of these photocatalysts. Therefore, the CDs 11 we screened is excellent photosensitizer, and the bonding between boron hydroxyl groups and zirconium clusters is essential, which greatly shortens the carrier migration distance and inhibits the recombination of photogenerated charges and holes.

We analyzed the photocatalyst through density functional theory (DFT) calculations to explore the factors contributing to the superior photocatalytic activity. Firstly, the optimized structural models of PCN-222 and CDs 11@PCN-222 were constructed by combining XPS and FTIR analysis ( Fig. 10a-b). It has been reported that the best adsorption site for oxygen is the ortho carbon atom of the benzene ring on the ligand in coordination with the zirconium cluster (C1 active site). And the best adsorption site for H+ is the oxygen atom of the C-O-Zr bond (O1 active site) [33]. Therefore, we calculated the adsorption energy of O2 and H+ on C1, O1 and multiple active sites adjacent to the B atom (C2, C3, O2, B) in the CDs 11@PCN-222. The results show that O2 adsorbs on the B site of the CDs 11@PCN-222, while for PCN-222, O2 is located at the C1 site (Fig. S11) [83]. The combination of the B-doped CDs 11 with PCN-222 effectively utilizes the delocalized π electrons in the benzene ring of CDs 11, which makes CDs 11@PCN-222 more conducive to O2 activation [33]. The adsorption energy of O2 on CDs 11@PCN-222 (B site) is −1.255 eV, which is much higher than that on PCN-222(C1 site) (-0.815 eV). The O2 adsorption isotherms and the temperature programmed desorption (TPD) of O2 measurement further verified this fact (Fig. 10c-d). The maximum O2 adsorption capacity of CDs 11@PCN-222 (43.02 cm3 g−1) is 4.1 times for PCN-222 (10.50 cm3 g−1), which is consistent with the TPD results. The strong O2 adsorption capacity of the CDs 11@PCN-222 is beneficial to the accumulation of O2 on its surface, thereby generating more superoxide radicals and realizing efficient H2O2 production. Hydrogen proton is an important part of the indirect 2e- ORR synthesis of H2O2, so the interaction of photocatalyst with H+ is crucial. The optimized adsorption configurations of H+ on active cites show that the best H+ adsorption sites of PCN-222 and CDs 11@PCN-222 are O1 and O2, and the corresponding adsorption energies are −0.581 eV and −1.373 eV (Fig. S12). Therefore, the CDs 11@PCN-222 is more conducive to the accumulation of H+, thereby converting O2 into H2O2. More importantly, the adsorption sites B and O2 of O2 and H+ on CDs 11@PCN-222 are closer than C1 and O1 on PCN-222, which indicates that CDs 11@PCN-222 is more favorable for the next proton coupling reactions (Eq. 3). This also explains why the CDs 11D@PCN-222 has excellent photocatalytic performance for H2O2 generation.

Photogenerated electron transfer is the key factor that directly determines the reaction mechanism and catalysis activity of binary hybrid catalyst in the photocatalytic process. Charge difference calculations confirmed a clear electron transfer between CDs 11 and PCN-222 ( Fig. 11a-b). Electronic charges of Zr (-1.97 e-), O2 (0.13 e-) and B (0.12 e-) atoms in B-O-Zr bond are described by the Bader charge, which proves the photogenerated electron transfer from PCN-222 to CDs. In addition, the B atom further becomes the active center due to the electron transfer between the electron-withdrawing hydroxyl group (B-OH) (0.09 e-) and the B atom, which is consistent with the reported conclusion [81]. The reaction potential barrier of O2 to OOH* directly determines the reaction rate. Therefore, the B active site in the CDs 11@PCN-222 can obtain the highest reaction rate, because the energy barrier that needs to be overcome in B center is the lowest compared with other active sites (Fig. 11c). Besides, according to the Sabatier principle [83], an ideal photocatalyst is necessary for proper adsorption of important intermediates or reaction substrates. The Ulimited (vs RHE) volcano map (overpotential of the volcano) as a function of Gibbs free energy for OOH* (*GOOH) (Fig. 11d) shows that the modification of the electronic structure of the B atom is conducive to reducing the adsorption of the intermediate product (OOH*), which facilitates the desorption of the H2O2 and inhibits the further decomposition of H2O2 by the photocatalyst [84]. Thus, too-strong or too-weak adsorption of the active site to OOH* can seriously hamper the ORR process. It is noticeable that B atom with better photocatalytic activity is located near the top of the volcano map, which is also consistent with above analyses.

In brief, the DFT and experimental results show that the as-designed and synthesized photocatalyst CDs 11@PCN-222 is reasonable and efficient. The reaction mechanism of 2e- ORR photosynthesis of H2O2 is further elucidated in detail in this work ( Fig. 12). When illuminated by Vis-NIR light, PCN-222 and CDs are excited, and the photogenerated electrons are transferred to the electron-deficient B active center through the zirconium-oxygen cluster. The strong adsorption of B site to oxygen promotes the generation of active species superoxide radicals from oxygen and electrons. The strong adsorption of hydrogen protons by O2 site in the B-O-Zr bond is conducive to the occurrence of further proton coupling, so that superoxide radicals and H+ generate intermediate OOH*. The weak adsorption of OOH* on the B active center makes the product H2O2 easy to desorb from the photocatalyst, which enhances the photocatalytic activity of the CDs 11@PCN-222. In addition, the presence of acidic aqueous solution or sodium ions can stabilize superoxide radicals and inhibit the decomposition of products. The excellent structure-activity relationship makes the photocatalyst unprecedented compared with the performance of currently reported catalysts (Table S4).

In summary, the B-doped CDs and PCN-222 were successfully utilized to construct a stable, broad light-absorbing, and efficient binary hybrid photocatalyst CDs 11D@PCN-222 through in situ encapsulation and bonding. The photocatalyst with highly efficient performance can be attributed to the following factors: (i) wide absorption edge for light; (ii) rapid separation of carriers and charge transfer; (iii) enhanced capacity for oxygen and H+ adsorption; (iv) appropriate adsorption for intermediate (OOH*) that inhibits H2O2 decomposition. The binary synergistic hybrid photocatalyst with wide absorption edges can achieve considerable H2O2 yields under Vis-NIR light, simulated sunlight, and even natural sunlight irradiation without the need for hole sacrificial agents. The introduction of B active site endows the CDs 11D@PCN-222 with strong oxygen and hydrogen proton adsorption, short-distance and efficient proton coupling. The photocatalyst can still maintain high stability in seawater after multiple cycles, and the photocatalytic activity will hardly be lost. Therefore, the photocatalyst CDs 11D@PCN-222 is suitable for seawater to generate a large amount of green oxidant H2O2 under the induction of light energy. This synthesis strategy is universal for the modification of most MOFs, and provides a reference for the synthesis process of green oxidants.
